author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him go now, and with panting lungs and swollen throat find fault with me as a mere dialectician.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Nay, let him summon, not me, but the science of dialectics itself, to the bar of popular opinion as a forger of lies, and let him open his mouth to its widest against it, with all the noisiest uproar of a special pleader.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him say whatever he pleases before the inexperienced, that so the learned may be moved to wrath, while the ignorant are deceived.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him call me, in virtue of my rhetoric, by the name of the orator Tertullus, by whom Paul was accused; and let him give himself the name of Advocate, in virtue of the pleading in which he boasts his former power, and for this reason delude himself with the notion that he is, or rather was, a namesake of the Holy Ghost.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him, with all my heart, exaggerate the foulness of the Manichæans, and endeavor to divert it on to me by his barking.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him quote all the exploits of those who have been condemned, whether known or unknown to me; and let him turn into the calumnious imputation of a prejudged crime, by some new right entirely his own, the fact that a former friend of mine there named me in my absence to the better securing of his own defense.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him read the titles that have been placed upon my letters by himself or by his friends, as suited their pleasure, and boast that he has, as it were, involved me hopelessly in their expressions.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: When I acknowledge certain eulogies of bread, uttered in all simplicity and merriment, let him take away my character with the absurd imputations of poisonous baseness and madness.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And let him entertain so bad an opinion of your understanding, as to imagine that he can be believed when he declares that pernicious love-charms were given to a woman, not only with the knowledge, but actually with the complicity of her husband.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What the man who was afterwards to ordain me bishop wrote about me in anger, while I was as yet a priest, he may freely seek to use as evidence against me.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: That the same man sought and obtained forgiveness from a holy Council for the wrong he thus had done me, he is equally at liberty to ignore as being in my favor,—being either so ignorant or so forgetful of Christian gentleness, and the commandment of the gospel, that he brings as an accusation against a brother what is wholly unknown to that brother himself, as he humbly entreats that pardon may in kindness be extended to him.
